name: New Jersey Dept of the Treasury
tagline: New Jersey's finance and administration hub: Treasury houses the Division of Purchase & Property — the state's central procurement shop that runs NJSTART and the statewide term contracts most NJ public buyers use — plus the Office of Management and Budget.
overview: The New Jersey Department of the Treasury is the state's finance and administration backbone. It houses the Office of Management and Budget (OMB), which builds and executes the state budget, and the Division of Purchase & Property (DPP), the central procurement agency that runs the NJSTART eProcurement portal and awards the statewide term contracts that state agencies, and many counties, municipalities, schools, and authorities, buy off of. For a technology seller, Treasury is best understood as the contracting path and the budget authority rather than the primary end user of IT — central IT strategy, the state data center, cybersecurity, and the NJ AI Assistant are run by a separate agency, the NJ Office of Information Technology (NJOIT), led by the Chief Technology Officer. The account sits inside a leadership transition: Governor Mikie Sherrill took office in January 2026 with a new Treasurer (Aaron Binder) and a new CTO (Kevin Dehmer), so buying centers and priorities are being reset across both Treasury and NJOIT.

budget.intro: Treasury owns the budget process through OMB, so its leverage is the whole state spending plan, not a single agency line. Governor Sherrill signed her inaugural FY2027 budget into law on June 30, 2026 at about $60.7B, with a surplus of roughly $6.08B; the bulk flows out as property-tax relief and aid to schools and local governments rather than direct state operations.

stats.label: FY2027 enacted budget
stats.note: Sherrill's first budget, signed June 30, 2026; ~$6.08B surplus

stats.label: Structural deficit (enacted)
stats.note: Cut to less than half the ~$3.0B projected when Sherrill took office

stats.label: Property-tax relief
stats.note: ANCHOR, Senior Freeze, Stay NJ — most spending is aid, not state IT

notes: Figures are from the enacted FY2027 Appropriations Act signed June 30, 2026; the plan includes limited IT/modernization line items (e.g., ~$13.3M for the NJ Innovation Authority permitting dashboard/Report Card and $3M for Consumer Affairs licensing-system upgrades), but most spending is aid, not state IT.
Agency-level IT spend lives in each department's budget; Treasury/DPP is the contracting vehicle and OMB the budget authority, not the wallet for most IT buys.

The Sherrill administration's stated themes are affordability, fiscal discipline (cost cuts of nearly $2B), and government efficiency/transparency — a frame for modernization and automation conversations.

initiatives.name: NJSTART eProcurement modernization
initiatives.summary: NJSTART is NJ's centralized, web-based procurement portal run by DPP — vendor registration, solicitations/RFPs, awarded term contracts, and electronic invoicing all flow through it. Being registered and correctly categorized in NJSTART is the precondition to selling to or through the state.

initiatives.name: NJ AI Assistant & responsible-AI push
initiatives.summary: NJ (via the NJ Innovation Authority with NJOIT) launched the NJ AI Assistant, a genAI environment for state employees, alongside an AI Task Force and statewide responsible-use policy and training. AI adoption is led outside Treasury at NJOIT/Innovation, but it signals demand for AI-ready infrastructure across state agencies.

initiatives.name: Digital government & efficiency agenda
initiatives.summary: The new administration is prioritizing permitting/licensing modernization, clearing backlogs, and cutting wait times — work that runs through NJOIT and individual agencies and is funded via OMB. Modernization and managed-services plays should map to these stated efficiency goals.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity posture (NJOIT / NJCCIC)
initiatives.summary: Statewide cyber is run outside Treasury — by NJOIT and the NJ Cybersecurity & Communications Integration Cell (NJCCIC) — with the CTO mandate to strengthen defenses against AI-driven threats. Treasury/DPP is the contracting path; the security buying center sits with NJOIT.

procurement.intro: Selling to New Jersey runs through DPP's central vehicles, not one-off agency RFPs in most cases. Two things to confirm first: an active, correctly scoped NJSTART vendor registration, and which statewide term contract(s) a Dell offering falls under. Cooperative purchasing also lets local governments, schools, and authorities ride state contracts.

vehicles.name: NJSTART vendor registration
vehicles.detail: The mandatory front door — vendors register and maintain a profile in NJSTART to bid, hold contracts, and invoice the state electronically. Confirm Dell's registration and categories are current.

vehicles.name: Statewide term contracts (DPP)
vehicles.detail: DPP awards master/term contracts that agencies buy off without separate RFPs. IT-relevant examples include M0483 (Computer Equipment & Peripherals), M4002 (Cloud & Software Solutions), and M4010 (Business & IT Consulting). Verify exact contract numbers/scope before forecasting.

vehicles.name: NJ Cooperative Purchasing Program
vehicles.detail: Lets registered counties, municipalities, school districts, and authorities purchase off state term contracts under N.J.A.C. 5:34 — extends a single state award across the broader NJ public sector.

powerStructure.name: Aaron Binder
powerStructure.title: State Treasurer
powerStructure.note: Sherrill-appointed Treasurer, unanimously confirmed by the Senate and sworn in March 4, 2026 (no longer acting); owns the FY2027 budget (signed June 30, 2026) and Treasury's divisions, including Purchase & Property and OMB. Top relationship for the finance/contracting motion. Deputy Treasurer is Kavin Mistry.

powerStructure.name: Andrea Spalla, Esq.
powerStructure.title: Assistant Treasurer (oversees Purchase & Property)
powerStructure.note: Continuing Assistant Treasurer under Binder; oversees the divisions of Purchase & Property, Pension & Benefits, and Revenue & Enterprise Services — a key path into the central procurement organization. Re-confirmed current as of June 2026.

powerStructure.name: Director, Division of Purchase & Property
powerStructure.title: Central procurement / NJSTART owner
powerStructure.note: Owns statewide term contracts, solicitations, and the NJSTART portal — the buying center for contracting. The named director is not consistently published; confirm directly before engagement (Amy Davis, Esq. appears in DPP leadership per public profiles, but the title is unverified).

powerStructure.name: Kevin Dehmer
powerStructure.title: Chief Technology Officer, NJOIT
powerStructure.note: Appointed Feb 2026; runs central IT, digital infrastructure, modernization, and cybersecurity OUTSIDE Treasury. The primary technology-strategy relationship — Treasury is the contracting path, NJOIT the IT decision-maker.

news.title: Gov. Sherrill signs FY2027 Appropriations Act (~$60.7B) into law on June 30
news.impact: Enacted budget: ~$6.08B surplus, structural deficit cut to ~$1.35B, sixth full pension payment ($7.3B), and limited IT/modernization items (~$13.3M NJ Innovation Authority permitting/Report Card, $3M Consumer Affairs licensing upgrades). Spend is now firm — forecast against enacted figures and the stated efficiency/modernization frame.

news.title: Treasurer Binder testifies to Senate Budget Committee: April revenue beat, added ~$50M in cuts
news.impact: Improved April collections plus additional spending reductions pushed the surplus toward ~$6B and the deficit under $1.5B before enactment — reinforces the disciplined-budgeting posture to position cost-saving modernization against.

news.title: Gov. Sherrill presents first FY2027 budget (~$60.7B); Treasurer Binder confirmed and sworn in
news.impact: Sets the affordability + efficiency frame; Binder unanimously Senate-confirmed and sworn in March 4 — the finance/contracting relationship is now settled, not acting.

news.title: Sherrill appoints Kevin Dehmer as state CTO (NJOIT)
news.impact: New owner of central IT, digital modernization, and cybersecurity strategy — rebuild the NJOIT relationship map; this is the IT decision-maker, distinct from Treasury contracting.

news.title: New Sherrill administration takes office; Aaron Binder sworn in as Treasurer
news.impact: Leadership reset across Treasury and its divisions — re-confirm contacts and priorities in Purchase & Property and OMB.

dellActions: Confirm Dell's NJSTART vendor registration is active and correctly categorized, and verify which DPP statewide term contracts (e.g., computer equipment, cloud/software, IT consulting) cover Dell's offerings — this is the access path to NJ state and cooperative buyers.
Run the technology motion through NJOIT and the CTO (Kevin Dehmer), not Treasury: Treasury/DPP is the contracting and budget authority, but central IT, AI, and cyber decisions sit at NJOIT.

Frame ISG/AI-infrastructure around the state's NJ AI Assistant and modernization agenda — position AI-ready compute and managed services as enablers of the efficiency goals the administration is funding.
Re-map relationships after the Jan 2026 transition: new Treasurer (Binder), new CTO (Dehmer), and confirm the current Director of Purchase & Property before forecasting.
Leverage the NJ Cooperative Purchasing Program to extend a single state term-contract award across counties, municipalities, schools, and authorities — broadening reach beyond state agencies.

sources.label: Gov. Sherrill signs FY2027 Appropriations Act (June 30, 2026)
Binder testifies on April revenue + belt-tightening (ROI-NJ, May 2026)
Sherrill appoints Kevin Dehmer as state CTO (NJOIT)
Aaron Binder sworn in as State Treasurer (NJ Globe)
NJ AI Task Force (NJ Innovation Authority)
NJ launches generative AI assistant for state employees (StateScoop)
